ground_truth.title: Best Audiobook Subscriptions in 2025 - I Tried 11 Services
ground_truth.author: Kristian Larsen
ground_truth.main_content: I discovered audiobooks while searching for a way to keep up with business books. Reading was taking too much time, and I had a growing stack of must-reads on my desk. That first audiobook turned into a second, then a third. Now I listen everywhere during commutes, while reviewing reports, and during email catch-up sessions. But when people ask about getting into audiobooks, they often hesitate at subscription prices. Which service is worth it? What about new features from Spotify? Should I just use a library card? After months of testing different platforms, I've found there's no one-size-fits-all answer - it really depends on how you like to listen. I tried 11 services: Audible (biggest library, $14.95/month with credits, owns 94 books), Libro.fm (supports local bookstores, good business curation), Blinkist (15-minute summaries of 6,500+ non-fiction titles, $12.99/month), Audiobooks.com (150,000+ titles, clean app, vague return policy), Downpour ($12.99/month with rental option, credits never expire), Everand (audiobooks plus ebooks, $12/month, hidden monthly limits), Epic (35,000+ titles, read-along features), GetAbstract (20,000+ business summaries, $299/year, professional narration), Kobo Audiobooks ($9.99/month with one credit), Apple Books (purchase individual audiobooks, seamless Apple sync), and Spotify Audiobooks (15 hours/month for Premium subscribers, familiar interface). For the largest library, Audible is hard to beat. For supporting local bookstores, Libro.fm delivers. Need unlimited listening? Everand works well if you can handle their monthly limits. For quick learning, I switch between Blinkist and GetAbstract. Spotify makes sense if you're already a Premium subscriber, while Kobo's lower price point makes it an attractive starting point. Most services offer 30-day trials, so you can test them risk-free.
with: I discovered audiobooks while searching for a way to keep up with business books.
But when people ask me about getting into audiobooks, they often hesitate at subscription prices.
After months of testing different platforms, I've found there's no one-size-fits-all answer - it really depends on how you like to listen.
One credit costs $14.95 monthly, and I use it for new releases that would normally cost $30-40.
Most services offer 30-day trials, so you can test them risk-free.
